thread-pool.name: Thread Pool
thread-pool.essence: Reuses a fixed set of worker threads
solves: spawning a thread per request works fine until traffic doubles and the box falls over
my process has thousands of threads and spends more time switching than working
memory blows up under load because every in-flight request carries its own stack
I need a hard ceiling on how much work runs at once instead of one per arrival
creating and tearing down a thread costs more than the tiny job I run on it

examples.name: java.util.concurrent.ExecutorService
examples.note: ThreadPoolExecutor and the Executors factory methods are the canonical fixed, cached, and work-stealing pools on the JVM. ThreadPoolExecutor exposes every knob directly — corePoolSize, maximumPoolSize, keepAliveTime, the BlockingQueue you hand it, and a pluggable RejectedExecutionHandler — which is why careful teams construct it explicitly rather than use Executors.newFixedThreadPool, whose convenience hides an unbounded queue.

examples.name: .NET ThreadPool
examples.note: The runtime keeps a managed, self-tuning pool of workers that backs Task.Run and async continuations, so user code rarely creates threads directly. It adjusts worker count with a hill-climbing heuristic and injects threads beyond the minimum only at a deliberately slow rate — so ThreadPool.SetMinThreads is the standard operational fix when bursts of blocking work make requests stall waiting for thread injection.

examples.name: libuv
examples.note: Node.js offloads filesystem, DNS lookups, and CPU-heavy crypto such as pbkdf2 to libuv's fixed-size worker pool so blocking calls never stall the event loop. The pool defaults to just 4 threads, sized once at startup via the UV_THREADPOOL_SIZE environment variable — the classic symptom of leaving it alone is fs and dns latency spiking together the moment a fifth blocking call queues.

future-promise.name: Future / Promise
future-promise.essence: A placeholder for a result that isn't ready yet
solves: my callbacks are nested five levels deep and I cannot follow the code anymore
the calling thread just sits there doing nothing while it waits for a response
I fire off ten independent API calls and they run one after another instead of at once
an error inside an async call gets swallowed and never reaches my error handling
I need to hand a not-yet-known result to another function without blocking on it first

examples.name: JavaScript Promise
examples.note: Standardized in ES2015 as the language-level settle-once handle that async/await desugars into, with all/race/allSettled as its combinators. Continuations always run on the microtask queue — never synchronously, even when the promise is already settled — and the safety net for swallowed errors is the unhandledRejection event, which Node.js turned into a process-crashing default in v15 precisely because vanishing rejections were the ecosystem's chronic bug.

examples.name: java.util.concurrent.CompletableFuture
examples.note: Merges the read-only Future handle and the resolvable producer side into one type you can chain with thenApply and thenCompose. Which thread runs each stage is the operational knob: the non-Async methods may execute on whichever thread completed the previous stage, while the *Async variants default to the common ForkJoinPool unless you pass an explicit Executor — and orTimeout/completeOnTimeout (Java 9+) are how a pending chain gets a deadline.

examples.name: .NET Task
examples.note: Task and Task<T> are the futures the C# async/await machinery is built on, resolved by the thread pool or by I/O completion. ConfigureAwait(false) is the dial that decides whether a continuation resumes on the captured context, cancellation flows through explicitly passed CancellationTokens rather than the handle itself, and blocking on .Result from a context the completion needs is the ecosystem's best-known deadlock.

I have a deadlock that only reproduces under load and I cannot pin it down

I need to model hundreds of thousands of stateful entities and a thread each is way too expensive
spreading my components across machines means rewriting how they talk to each other

examples.name: Erlang/OTP
examples.note: BEAM processes are share-nothing actors, and OTP supervision trees restart a crashed one instead of defending against every failure in-line. The supervisor exposes the knobs directly — one_for_one vs. rest_for_one restart strategies and a restart-intensity cap that escalates a crash loop up the tree — and the scheduler preempts processes on a reduction budget, so no single actor can hog a core.

examples.name: Akka
examples.note: Brought Erlang-style actors, supervision, and location transparency to the JVM, with clustering that lets the same actor code span nodes. Dispatchers and mailbox types are swap-in configuration rather than code — the standard operational move is giving blocking work its own dispatcher so it cannot starve the default one — and Cluster Sharding spreads entity actors across nodes while passivating the idle ones.

examples.name: Microsoft Orleans
examples.note: Its virtual actors (grains) are activated on first message and deactivated when idle, so placement and lifecycle stay the runtime's problem. A grain processes one request at a time unless explicitly marked reentrant, and the idle-deactivation age is a configurable dial — operators tune how long a silent grain holds memory rather than where it lives.

I want to watch a pile of sockets and timers in one place without a thread for each

examples.name: AWS IAM

secure-session-manager.name: Secure Session Manager
secure-session-manager.essence: Issues, tracks, and expires session state safely
solves: logging out does not actually stop the old cookie from working
someone grabbed a cookie off a shared machine and it still gets them in months later
a compromised account stays logged in and I have no way to kick it out before the token expires
users get signed out at random whenever their request lands on a different server
I keep asking people to log in again on every single page because nothing remembers them

relations.note: Authenticate, then carry a secure session
relations.name: Authentication Enforcer
relations.label: Combines with

examples.name: Django session framework
examples.note: Issues a random session key in a cookie while keeping the session data server-side, with configurable expiry and rotation on login.

examples.name: Spring Session
examples.note: Externalizes HTTP session state into Redis or a database so any application instance can validate and revoke the same session.

examples.name: express-session
examples.note: Middleware that mints signed session cookies backed by a pluggable server-side store and regenerates the id on demand.

intercepting-validator.name: Intercepting Validator
intercepting-validator.essence: Validates and sanitizes input before it reaches logic
solves: a user typed a quote character into a search box and broke everything downstream
every controller starts with twenty lines checking that fields are not empty or too long
a request with a missing field exploded deep in the domain code with a useless stack trace
we tightened a length limit on one endpoint and forgot the three others taking the same payload
people skip my nice client-side checks by calling the API directly with curl

relations.note: Screen before forwarding
relations.name: Gatekeeper
relations.label: Combines with

relations.note: Validate input at the edge
relations.name: API Gateway
relations.label: Combines with

themes.name: Auth & Access
themes.role: Reject bad input before it reaches logic

examples.name: Pydantic in FastAPI
examples.note: Parses and validates each request body against a declared model before the endpoint function is ever called, rejecting bad input with a 422.

examples.name: Hibernate Validator (Bean Validation)
examples.note: Applies declarative constraint annotations to request objects, checked at the controller boundary before the method body runs.

examples.name: express-validator
examples.note: Composes validation and sanitization rules into a middleware chain that runs ahead of the route handler.

secure-logger.name: Secure Logger
secure-logger.essence: Records events for audit without leaking sensitive data
solves: we found customer passwords sitting in plaintext in our log search tool
a stack trace dumped the whole request object including a card number
an auditor asked who opened this record back in March and I have nothing to show them
our logs ship to a third-party service and I cannot promise there are no secrets in them
anyone with database access could quietly edit the history to hide what they did

relations.note: Tap to an audit log
relations.name: Wire Tap
relations.label: Combines with

examples.name: AWS CloudTrail
examples.note: Records every API call as an append-only audit trail and offers log file integrity validation, hashing and signing digests so tampering is detectable.

examples.name: Pino (redact option)
examples.note: Takes a configured list of field paths and replaces their values with a placeholder as the log line is serialized, so secrets never reach the sink.

examples.name: Linux auditd
examples.note: Funnels security-relevant kernel events into one access-controlled audit log intended for later forensic review.

single-access-point.name: Single Access Point
single-access-point.essence: One well-guarded entry, instead of many small ones
solves: every service has its own login screen and one of them is definitely out of date
we patched a hole in one service and then found the same hole in four others
an internal admin tool is reachable from the internet and nobody remembers opening it
auditors want proof that every inbound request is logged and I cannot give it to them
each team opens its own port and I have lost track of what is actually reachable from outside

relations.note: One sign-on across many services

examples.name: Cloudflare Access
examples.note: Puts one authenticated proxy in front of internal applications so they never listen on the public internet directly.

examples.name: Teleport
examples.note: Acts as the single hardened proxy through which all SSH, Kubernetes, and database access to a protected fleet must pass, logging every session.

examples.name: Kong Gateway
examples.note: Fronts a fleet of services with one entry point that applies authentication, rate limiting, and logging before routing.

least-privilege.name: Least Privilege
least-privilege.essence: Every component gets the minimum access it needs, no more
solves: one leaked API key gave an attacker everything we own
every service runs with the same admin credentials because that was the quickest way to ship
our CI job can deploy to production and also read every customer database
nobody can tell me why this role has these permissions or whether anyone still needs them
a compromised container reached services it had no business touching

relations.note: Grant only what the role needs
